section.title: The Decree of Cyrus
section.sectionRationale: The book opens by answering a question the exile had left hanging: is God's word finished, or will He keep His promise to bring His people home? In the first year of Cyrus the LORD moves a pagan emperor to release the exiles and fund the rebuilding of His house, and He restores the very vessels Nebuchadnezzar had carried off. It is one movement: the word of God, the stirred king, the willing people, and the returned treasures.
section.thesis: God keeps His word by ruling the heart of a Persian king, stirring the spirits of His people, and restoring true worship, so that the return from exile begins not with human strength but with the sovereign, faithful hand of God.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the LORD stirs Cyrus to decree the return, verses 1 to 4; the family heads and priests are stirred to go up, verses 5 and 6; and the holy vessels are counted out and carried back to Jerusalem, verses 7 to 11.

units.label: The LORD stirs Cyrus to decree the return
units.note: to fulfill the word of the LORD

verses.text: In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, to fulfill the word of the LORD spoken through Jeremiah, the LORD {{uwr|stirred}} the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia to send a proclamation throughout his kingdom and to put it in writing as follows:
“This is what Cyrus king of Persia says: ‘The LORD, the God of heaven, who has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, has appointed me to build a house for Him at Jerusalem in Judah.
Whoever among you belongs to His people, may his God be with him, and may he go to Jerusalem in Judah and build the house of the LORD, the God of Israel; He is the God who is in Jerusalem.

And let every {{sheerit|survivor}}, wherever he lives, be assisted by the men of that region with silver, gold, goods, and livestock, along with a {{nedabah|freewill offering}} for the house of God in Jerusalem.’”

units.label: The people are stirred to go up
units.note: everyone whose spirit God had stirred

verses.text: So the family heads of Judah and Benjamin, along with the priests and Levites, everyone whose spirit God had stirred, prepared to go up and rebuild the {{bayit|house}} of the LORD in Jerusalem.
And all their neighbors supported them with articles of silver and gold, with goods and livestock, and with valuables, in addition to all their freewill offerings.
King Cyrus also brought out the articles belonging to the house of the LORD that Nebuchadnezzar had carried away from Jerusalem and placed in the temple of his gods.
Cyrus king of Persia had them brought out by the hand of Mithredath the treasurer, who counted them out to Sheshbazzar the prince of Judah.
This was the inventory: 30 gold dishes, 1,000 silver dishes, 29 silver utensils,
30 gold bowls, 410 matching silver bowls, and 1,000 other articles.
In all, there were 5,400 gold and silver articles. Sheshbazzar brought all these along when the exiles went up from Babylon to Jerusalem.

uwr.gloss: To rouse, awaken, or stir up. The same verb frames the whole chapter: the LORD stirs the spirit of Cyrus in verse 1 and stirs the spirits of His people in verse 5. God does not merely predict the return; He inwardly moves both a pagan king and His own people to accomplish it.

sheerit.gloss: One who is left, a survivor or remnant. Those who return are the leftover ones, the surviving remnant of a nation that had been all but swept away. The word carries the hope that runs through the prophets: God always preserves a remnant through judgment.

nedabah.gloss: A freewill offering, a gift given willingly rather than commanded. The return and the rebuilding are funded by glad, uncoerced giving, an early mark of hearts that God Himself has stirred to seek His house.

bayit.gloss: House, and here the house of the LORD, the temple. The whole book turns on this bayit: God's dwelling among His people. Its rebuilding points beyond stone to the true temple, Christ, and to the church He indwells by His Spirit.

basic.context: The book of Ezra opens about fifty years after Babylon destroyed Jerusalem and carried Judah into exile. Jeremiah had promised the captivity would last seventy years and then end. Here, in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, God keeps that promise: He moves Cyrus to issue a decree letting the exiles go home and rebuild the temple, and He even returns the sacred vessels Nebuchadnezzar had stolen. The key question is simple: who is really running this? The answer is God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history, and it is told to show God's hand behind world events. Cyrus was a real emperor with his own political reasons for his policy, yet the text says the LORD stirred his spirit to fulfill the word spoken through Jeremiah. We should read the decree as fulfilled prophecy, not as a model for how to run governments. The abiding truth is that God keeps His promises and rules the hearts of kings.
basic.originalLanguages: One verb ties the chapter together: God stirred, ur, the spirit of Cyrus, and He stirred the spirits of His people. Those who come home are the survivors, the remnant. And they give freewill offerings, nedabah, gladly, toward the house, the bayit, of the LORD.
basic.typology: The return from exile is a picture of a greater homecoming. The temple being rebuilt points forward to Christ, the true temple, and to the church. The stirring of a pagan king to serve God's people foreshadows the day when the nations bring their glory into the city of God.
basic.christ: God moved an unbelieving emperor to release His people and restore true worship. In the same way, God works through all of history, even through those who do not know Him, to bring about the redemption that comes to a head in Jesus. The little return under Cyrus is a down payment on the great return from the exile of sin that Christ accomplishes.
basic.god: God is sovereign and faithful. He rules the most powerful ruler on earth as easily as a stream of water, and He keeps the promise He made through Jeremiah down to the year. He is also the God who stirs hearts, moving people from the inside to seek Him.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 29:10 promised seventy years and then a return. Isaiah 44:28 named Cyrus by name long before he was born. 2 Chronicles 36:22-23 tells the same decree. And Proverbs 21:1 says the king's heart is a stream the LORD turns wherever He wills.
basic.oneStory: The exile was the low point of Israel's story, and it looked like the promises had failed. Chapter one is God saying no: His word stands. He brings a remnant home to rebuild His house, and that homecoming is a small, true picture of the great homecoming He will accomplish in Christ.

context.literary: Ezra opens with an almost verbatim echo of the last verses of 2 Chronicles, stitching the story of the return onto the story of the exile. The chapter moves in three quick scenes, the decree, the stirred people, the restored vessels, and it is dominated by the repeated verb stirred and by careful counting, a narrator's way of showing that nothing here is accidental.
context.historical: Cyrus the Great captured Babylon in 539 BC and, unlike the Babylonians, made a policy of returning displaced peoples and their gods to their homelands. His decree fits what we know of Persian practice, yet Scripture reads a deeper cause behind the political one: the LORD was fulfilling His word. Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah, leads the first group and receives the temple vessels by inventory.
context.canonical: This is the beginning of the post-exilic era and the fulfillment of the prophets' promise of restoration. It sets up the whole book of Ezra and Nehemiah, and it stands in the larger biblical pattern of exile and return that reaches its climax when Christ brings His people out of the deeper exile of sin and death.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the return from exile was God's doing, the faithful fulfillment of His promise through Jeremiah, accomplished by His sovereign rule over Cyrus and His inward stirring of the remnant to rebuild His house.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, once-for-all act of God in history; it does not prescribe that believers expect governments to fund their worship. What endures and instructs us is God's faithfulness to His word, His sovereignty over rulers, and His work of stirring hearts to seek Him.

debates.question: In what sense did the LORD stir the spirit of Cyrus?

views.label: God worked through Cyrus's own motives
views.case: Cyrus had political and religious reasons of his own for repatriating peoples; God sovereignly governed those very motives to accomplish His purpose, so the stirring is real without overriding Cyrus's will.

views.label: God directly moved Cyrus's heart
views.case: The text credits the LORD as the cause and links it to fulfilling prophecy; Proverbs 21:1 says God turns a king's heart like a stream, so the emphasis falls on God's direct, effective working within the king.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold the main point: the decree was ultimately God's act, not merely Persian policy, which is why the exiles could trust that the God who began the return would see it through.

debates.question: Was Cyrus a true believer in the LORD?

views.label: A pagan king used by God
views.case: Cyrus honored many gods and speaks of the LORD as one deity among the peoples he served; his confession is diplomatic, and Isaiah 45:4-5 says God calls him though he does not know Him.

views.label: A king given real light
views.case: His words in verses 2 and 3 confess the LORD as the God of heaven who gave him his kingdoms and appointed him to build the temple, which sounds like genuine, if limited, acknowledgment.

debates.bearing: Either way the lesson holds: God can accomplish the good of His people through rulers who do not fully know Him, so His purposes never depend on the faith of kings.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on one verb, ur, to stir or rouse. In verse 1 the LORD stirs the spirit of Cyrus; in verse 5 He stirs the spirit of everyone who goes up. The same divine action moves the emperor on his throne and the family head in his home. Those who respond are the sheerit, the survivors, the remnant left after judgment, and they bring the nedabah, the freewill offering, glad gifts toward the bayit, the house of the LORD. The vocabulary quietly makes the point of the whole book: the restoration of God's dwelling among His people is God's own work, from the stirring of hearts to the funding of the house.

typology.type: The return from exile to the land
typology.fulfillment: The greater deliverance Christ accomplishes, bringing His people home from the exile of sin, Luke 4:18-21 and 1 Peter 2:9-10.

typology.type: The rebuilt house of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true temple where God dwells with man, and the church built into a temple by the Spirit, John 2:19-21 and Ephesians 2:19-22.

typology.type: A pagan king stirred to serve God's people
typology.fulfillment: The nations bringing their glory into the city of God, Isaiah 60:3-11 and Revelation 21:24-26.

study.christ: The whole scene is about God keeping a promise no human power could keep, and doing it by ruling the heart of the mightiest king on earth. That is the pattern of the gospel. Just as God moved Cyrus to release captives and restore true worship, so in the fullness of time God worked through rulers who did not know Him, even through Caesar's decree and Pilate's court, to accomplish the redemption of His people in Christ. The temple that Cyrus funds points to Jesus, the true temple, in whom God dwells and through whom we are brought near. The remnant brought home is a small picture of the great homecoming Christ secures for all who trust Him.
study.god: God is utterly sovereign: He holds the heart of Cyrus in His hand and turns it to fulfill a word spoken generations earlier. God is faithful: the seventy years Jeremiah foretold end exactly as promised, so His people learn that not one of His words falls to the ground. And God is the One who works within: He does not merely command the return, He stirs the spirits of survivors and family heads so that they willingly rise up to rebuild. The same God who moves empires moves individual hearts to seek Him.

crossReferences.note: The promise of seventy years in Babylon followed by a return, which this decree fulfills.
Cyrus named and commissioned by God long before his birth to rebuild Jerusalem and free the exiles.
The same decree of Cyrus, forming the seam between the exile and the return.
The king's heart is a stream of water in the LORD's hand; He turns it wherever He wills.

study.oneStory: Exile looked like the end of the promises. Chapter one answers that fear: the word of the LORD through Jeremiah comes true to the year, God stirs a pagan king and a willing remnant, and the treasures of true worship are carried home. This modest return is a real down payment on the great return from exile that Jesus accomplishes, when God brings His people, funded and secured entirely by His grace, home to Himself forever.

quiz.q: Why does the text say Cyrus issued his decree?

options.text: Because the LORD stirred his spirit, to fulfill the word spoken through Jeremiah.
options.note: Verse 1 gives God's action and the fulfillment of prophecy as the true cause.

options.text: Because it was standard Persian policy to send peoples home.
options.note: Persian policy was the human means, but the text names God's stirring as the deeper reason.

options.text: Because the exiles had grown powerful enough to demand it.
options.note: The exiles were a small remnant with no power to demand anything.

options.text: Because Cyrus had become a devout worshiper of the LORD alone.
options.note: Isaiah 45:4-5 says God used Cyrus though he did not truly know Him.

quiz.q: What promise was being fulfilled in this chapter?

options.text: Jeremiah's word that after seventy years in Babylon God would bring His people home.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly links the decree to the word through Jeremiah, see Jeremiah 29:10.

options.text: The promise that Israel would never again be conquered.
options.note: No such promise is in view; the point is the promised return after exile.

options.text: A promise made by Cyrus to his own gods.
options.note: The fulfilled word is God's promise through Jeremiah, not a Persian vow.

options.text: There was no prior promise; this was simply good fortune.
options.note: The text roots the event in God's prophetic word, not chance.

quiz.q: What does the repeated word stirred tell us about the return?

options.text: God moved both the king and His people from within, so the return was His work in every part.
options.note: The verb ur is used of Cyrus in verse 1 and the people in verse 5.

options.text: The people worked up their own enthusiasm to go home.
options.note: Human willingness was real, but the text credits God's stirring as its source.

options.text: Only Cyrus was moved; the people were forced to go.
options.note: The people went willingly, their spirits also stirred by God.

options.text: It tells us nothing in particular; it is just narration.
options.note: The deliberate repetition makes God's inward working the theme of the chapter.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter carefully inventory the temple vessels?

options.text: To show God restoring true worship, returning the very articles Nebuchadnezzar had stolen.
options.note: The counted vessels signal the recovery of the LORD's house and worship.

options.text: To prove the exiles were wealthy.
options.note: The vessels belong to the LORD's house, not to private wealth; the point is restored worship.

options.text: To record a business transaction with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The careful count underscores God's faithful restoration, not mere bookkeeping.

options.text: To show the exiles had made new idols.
options.note: These are the holy vessels of the LORD, being restored, not idols.

quiz.q: How does the decree of Cyrus point us toward Christ?

options.text: God rules even unbelieving rulers to free His people and restore worship, as He does supremely in the redemption Christ brings.
options.note: The return foreshadows the greater deliverance and the true temple in Jesus.

options.text: It shows that political power is the way God saves.
options.note: God uses rulers, but salvation comes through Christ, not political power itself.

options.text: It teaches that only kings can do God's work.
options.note: God stirs kings and ordinary people alike; the work is His.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The return from exile is a key thread that runs to Christ's greater rescue.

questions: God kept His word through Jeremiah down to the year, even when the exile looked final. Where do you need to trust that a promise of God still stands, though circumstances say otherwise?
The LORD turned the heart of the most powerful man on earth to serve His people. How does God's rule over rulers steady you when you look at the leaders and headlines of your own day?
God stirred the spirits of ordinary family heads to rise up and rebuild. Where might God be stirring you to give yourself to His work, and what is your response?
The exiles gave freewill offerings gladly toward the house of God. What would glad, uncoerced generosity toward God's work look like in your life right now?
The return from exile is a small picture of the great homecoming Christ secures. How does knowing your true home is guaranteed in Jesus change the way you live as an exile here?

translationNotes.note: To fulfill the word of the LORD spoken through Jeremiah refers to the seventy-year prophecy of Jeremiah 25:11-12 and 29:10; the same verb rendered stirred is used again in verse 5 of the people.

Sheshbazzar the prince of Judah leads the first return; the BSB footnote explains the phrase means the leader of the exiles returning to Judah. He is later distinguished in the narrative from Zerubbabel.
The numbers and terms for the dishes and utensils are technical and slightly uncertain in the Hebrew; the totals are given to stress the full restoration of the LORD's holy vessels.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 1:1-11. Em dashes in the source (for example verse 5) were rendered as commas per house style; no dashes remain.
